solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public String minWindow(String s, String t) {
        if (s == null || s.length() == 0 || t == null || t.length() == 0) return "";

        int[] tCount = new int[128];
        int[] windowCount = new int[128];

        for (char c : t.toCharArray()) {
            tCount[c]++;
        }

        int required = 0;
        for (int i : tCount) {
            if (i > 0) required++;
        }

        int formed = 0, left = 0, right = 0, minLen = Integer.MAX_VALUE;
        int[] result = new int[]{-1, -1};

        while (right < s.length()) {
            char c = s.charAt(right);
            windowCount[c]++;

            if (tCount[c] > 0 && windowCount[c] == tCount[c]) {
                formed++;
            }

            while (left <= right && formed == required) {
                if (right - left + 1 < minLen) {
                    minLen = right - left + 1;
                    result[0] = left;
                    result[1] = right;
                }

                char leftChar = s.charAt(left);
                windowCount[leftChar]--;
                if (tCount[leftChar] > 0 && windowCount[leftChar] < tCount[leftChar]) {
                    formed--;
                }
                left++;
            }
            right++;
        }

        return minLen == Integer.MAX_VALUE ? "" : s.substring(result[0], result[1] + 1);
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(m + n)
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses the sliding window technique with two pointers to efficiently find the minimum window substring. It maintains counts of characters in the current window and compares them to the required counts from string `t`. The algorithm ensures linear time complexity by avoiding nested loops and only traversing the input string once.
